what_it_does: Acceptance test harness for the runtime.rules engine (G3/L2 core). It exercises declared data-AST predicates, deterministic evaluation, adversarial rejection of nondeterminism/IO/model calls, loud failure on missing inputs, routing to five destinations (inject/chain/address/surface/lane), the surface path's inability to forge resolve/approve/dispatch, renderable/addressable rule records, a nesting depth cap, the static AST walk as a commit gate for role rules, and a drift-home check that RULE_OPS and DESTINATION_KINDS are reflected in runtime/AGENTS.md.
observations: Uses a module-level PASS counter plus check() and expect_raise() helpers instead of a standard test framework.
Constructs a rich AST using op values and, field, ge, in, and lit to test boolean/comparison/membership predicates.
Tests determinism by evaluating the same resolved input 100 times and by comparing results across different dict insertion orders.
Compares the new declared injection_rule against an old hand-written reference function for parity.
Tokenizes runtime/rules.py to structurally prove there is no eval/exec/compile/ast.parse call in the engine source.
Tests adversarial rejection of now, random, call, read_file, finish_order, dunder field paths, and non-literal literals.
Demonstrates on_missing='skip' as an explicit no-op with a reason, not an implicit truthy fallback.
Uses both a FakeSuite and the real Suite to verify that surface_review emits ask events and keeps resolved=None.
Exercises all five destinations by actual routing and checks that forbidden destinations are rejected at build time.
Verifies drift home by scanning runtime/AGENTS.md for every RULE_OPS entry and DESTINATION_KINDS entry.
standouts: Adversarial static-whitelist tests that reject time/random/IO/model/order-reading ops at AST validation.
Surface destination is proven to be an escalation (ask event, resolved=None) and structurally unable to emit resolve/approve/dispatch.
All five routing destinations are demonstrated by use, including chain execution via a stub run_role executor.
Structural source-token check that the rule engine never parses or evaluates strings.
conventions: Inline test harness with global mutable state rather than unittest/pytest classes.
Positive assertions and adversarial assertions are paired for each requirement area.
Imports are scattered throughout the file near the sections that need them.
Fake collaborators (FakeSuite, fake_chain_executor, emit) stand in for real runtime objects.
Comments map every test block to a C3.x requirement id.
concerns: Global PASS counter and side-effect assertion helpers make the test stateful and harder to compose.
Mid-file imports and conditional discovery make the file's dependencies less visible at the top.
The structural regex and tokenization checks are brittle and may break with harmless source reformatting.
Tests depend on external directories (roles/, nodes/, runtime/AGENTS.md) and will fail if those drift.
The file is long and mixes many concerns (unit, integration, structural, documentation) in one module.
notes: This file is an executable acceptance spec for a restricted, deterministic rule engine with adversarial safety tests and a drift-home registry check.
questions: What is the exact value of MAX_RULE_DEPTH and why was it chosen?
What are the full semantics of on_missing beyond 'skip' and 'fail'?
How does validate_role_rules distinguish AST-shaped rules from descriptive rules?
What contract does route() expect from a chain_executor and how does it store the returned value?
What is the intended format of runtime/AGENTS.md for drift-home checks?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the docstring says it proves G3 by use and adversarial testing, and the body does exactly that.
apparent_themes: normal change path as commit gate
intention_signals: It is trying to make the rule layer auditable and safe by construction: a small declared language, no eval path, deterministic routing, explicit destinations, and tests that double as a specification and commit gate.
novelty: Structural proof by tokenizing runtime/rules.py to show no eval/exec/compile/ast.parse calls; treating acceptance tests as the executable spec for a forbidden-dispatch floor; and verifying registry drift home inside the same test file.
contribution: An acceptance-test spec that defines expected behavior of the rule engine, including positive use cases, adversarial cases, destination semantics, surface safety, depth/renderability limits, and the role-discovery commit gate; it also provides fake collaborators and a drift-home check that can be reused or extended.
summary_for_embedding: Acceptance tests for the G3 rule engine covering declared-AST evaluation, determinism, adversarial rejection, five routing destinations, surface safety, renderability, depth limits, commit gating, and drift-home registry verification.
intention_for_embedding: To prove by use and adversarial testing that the rule engine is deterministic, restricted, renderable, and cannot forge resolve/approve/dispatch events.

symbols.does: Test helper that asserts a condition and increments a global pass counter.
symbols.description: check(label, cond) asserts cond is truthy, increments the module-level PASS counter, and prints an ok line; it is the basic positive assertion primitive used throughout the file.

symbols.does: Test helper that asserts a callable raises a given exception.
symbols.description: expect_raise(label, fn, exc=RuleError) calls fn, expects exc to be raised, increments PASS on success, and fails if no exception occurs; it is used for adversarial rejection tests.

symbols.does: Reference hand-written rule logic used for parity comparison.
symbols.description: old_injection(resolved) implements the prior imperative injection decision based on recall.relevant AND ground.in_scope so the test can compare it field-by-field against the new declared AST rule.

symbols.does: Minimal stand-in for the real Suite to capture surface behavior.
symbols.description: FakeSuite records events and surfaced items, providing a surface_review method that emits an ask event and returns an item with resolved=None, mirroring the real contract without a live registry or store.

symbols.does: Initializes empty event and surfaced lists.
symbols.description: Sets self.events and self.surfaced to empty lists so subsequent surface_review calls can be inspected by tests.

symbols.does: Records an event tuple for later inspection.
symbols.description: _emit(kind, summary, **meta) appends a (kind, payload) tuple to self.events; it is used by FakeSuite.surface_review to capture the ask event.

symbols.does: Simulates surfacing a review item without resolving it.
symbols.description: surface_review(item, origin='responsive') creates a surfaced item with resolved=None, records it, emits an ask event, and returns an inbox status; it models the real surface_review contract used by the surface destination.

symbols.does: Global event recorder for lane destination tests.
symbols.description: emit(kind, payload) appends (kind, payload) to the module-level emitted list so tests can assert that lane rules wrote cognition.lane events.

symbols.does: Stub executor used to test the chain destination.
symbols.description: fake_chain_executor(role_id) records that the role was fired and returns a dummy CheckOut-shaped result, letting the test verify that route triggers the dependent role and lands its output at a run:// address.

symbols.does: Builds a nested AST of a given depth for depth-cap tests.
symbols.description: deep_ast(n) wraps a field node in n successive not operations, producing an AST whose depth can be tuned to test validation below and above MAX_RULE_DEPTH.
